Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right).

\text{Based on the domain of } \tan^{-1} \text{ and the sign of the argument, } \alpha \text{ is in Quadrant } I.

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = 16, \quad y = 12, \quad r = 20

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{3}{5}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{4}{5}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = \cos^2(\alpha) - \sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = \left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^2

\cos(2\alpha) = \frac{7}{25}

Solution:

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = -2, \quad y = 11, \quad r = 5 \, \sqrt{5}

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{11 \, \sqrt{5}}{25}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = -\frac{2 \, \sqrt{5}}{25}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = \cos^2(\alpha) - \sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = \left(-\frac{2 \, \sqrt{5}}{25}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{11 \, \sqrt{5}}{25}\right)^2

\cos(2\alpha) = -\frac{117}{125}

\sin(\alpha) = -\frac{5 \, \sqrt{349}}{349}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{18 \, \sqrt{349}}{349}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(-\frac{5 \, \sqrt{349}}{349}\right)\left(\frac{18 \, \sqrt{349}}{349}\right)

\sin(2\alpha) = -\frac{180}{349}

\text{Based on the domain of } \csc^{-1} \text{ and the sign of the argument, } \alpha \text{ is in Quadrant } I.

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = 7, \quad y = 16, \quad r = \sqrt{305}

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{16 \, \sqrt{305}}{305}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{7 \, \sqrt{305}}{305}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = \cos^2(\alpha) - \sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = \left(\frac{7 \, \sqrt{305}}{305}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{16 \, \sqrt{305}}{305}\right)^2

\cos(2\alpha) = -\frac{207}{305}

\text{Based on the domain of } \cos^{-1} \text{ and the sign of the argument, } \alpha \text{ is in Quadrant } I.

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = 18, \quad y = 20, \quad r = 2 \, \sqrt{181}

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{10 \, \sqrt{181}}{181}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{9 \, \sqrt{181}}{181}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{10 \, \sqrt{181}}{181}\right)\left(\frac{9 \, \sqrt{181}}{181}\right)

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{180}{181}

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = 11, \quad y = -3, \quad r = \sqrt{130}

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = -\frac{3 \, \sqrt{130}}{130}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{11 \, \sqrt{130}}{130}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(-\frac{3 \, \sqrt{130}}{130}\right)\left(\frac{11 \, \sqrt{130}}{130}\right)

\sin(2\alpha) = -\frac{33}{65}

\text{Based on the domain of } \cos^{-1} \text{ and the sign of the argument, } \alpha \text{ is in Quadrant } I.

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = 20, \quad y = 6, \quad r = 2 \, \sqrt{109}

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{3 \, \sqrt{109}}{109}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{10 \, \sqrt{109}}{109}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = \cos^2(\alpha) - \sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = \left(\frac{10 \, \sqrt{109}}{109}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{3 \, \sqrt{109}}{109}\right)^2

\cos(2\alpha) = \frac{91}{109}

\text{Using the reference triangle, we identify: } x = 18, \quad y = 12, \quad r = 6 \, \sqrt{13}

\text{Evaluate the fundamental trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{2 \, \sqrt{13}}{13}, \quad \cos(\alpha) = \frac{3 \, \sqrt{13}}{13}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{2 \, \sqrt{13}}{13}\right)\left(\frac{3 \, \sqrt{13}}{13}\right)

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{12}{13}
